name: Devoir & Il Faut — Must / Have To

cards.grammarNote: devoir + infinitive = must / have to. Conjugation: je dois, tu dois, il doit, nous devons, vous devez, ils doivent. se reposer = to rest (reflexive).
devoir can mean 'should' (moral/practical obligation) or 'must' (strong necessity) depending on context. In health advice, 'tu dois' carries the meaning 'you should / you need to'.
il faut + infinitive = it is necessary to / one must. il faut is impersonal — it has no specific subject. il faut is often stronger and more formal than devoir.

cards.front: I had to go to the hospital
cards.grammarNote: Past tense of devoir: j'ai dû (passé composé) = I had to. Irregular past participle: dû (with circumflex on u to distinguish from du, the article).
il doit = he must (third person singular). au lit = in bed (à + le = au). rester au lit = to stay in bed.
il faut + infinitive with an adverb: bien manger (eat well). il faut gives general advice without specifying who — more universal than nous devons.

cards.front: You don't have to work today
cards.back: Tu n'as pas besoin de travailler aujourd'hui
cards.grammarNote: ne pas avoir besoin de + infinitive = don't have to / don't need to. Contrast with ne pas devoir = must not (prohibition), which is much stronger.

cards.back: Il ne faut pas fumer ici
cards.grammarNote: il ne faut pas + infinitive = you must not / it is not allowed to. This is a prohibition, not just a suggestion. Much stronger than il ne faut pas in advisory context.

cards.front: She had to rest for a week
cards.back: Elle a dû se reposer pendant une semaine
cards.grammarNote: elle a dû = she had to (passé composé of devoir). pendant = for (a duration in the past). The infinitive following dû stays in its base form.
je devrais = I should (conditional of devoir). The conditional makes the advice gentler and less absolute than je dois. davantage = more (used without a direct noun, unlike plus).

cards.front: It is necessary to drink water
cards.grammarNote: il faut + infinitive for general health rules. de l'eau = partitive article (some water). This impersonal construction is very common in instructions and advice.

cards.front: I have to take it twice a day
cards.back: Je dois le prendre deux fois par jour
cards.grammarNote: le (direct object pronoun) replaces the medicine already mentioned. par jour = per day. deux fois = twice.
